coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot embedded polyaniline for the fabrication of high-performance flexible supercapacitor with enhanced cycling stability
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Punnakkal, Navaneeth
Naneena, S
C P, Shyam Lal
Pradeep, Aarathi
T G, Satheesh Babu
Suneesh, Punathil Vasu
coredata.dc:description: This article explains the synthesis of high surface area nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded polyaniline (PANI) and its effectiveness in fabricating flexible printed supercapacitors. A noticeable change in polyaniline's morphology and specific surface area suggests the successful incorporation of graphene quantum dots into the polymer matrix. The synergistic interaction of polyaniline with graphene quantum dots is evident in its energy storage performance. The fabricated supercapacitor prototype with PVA/HCl gel electrolyte exhibited a very high areal capacitance of 128.01 mF cm−2, with an areal energy density of 11.40 μWh cm−2, and a power density of 640 μW cm−2 at 1.6 mA cm−2 current density. Most importantly, the developed supercapacitor exhibited 76.70% of original capacitance after 5000 galvanostatic charge-discharge cycles, which was found to be far better result than a supercapacitor fabricated with pristine polyaniline. Furthermore, the developed supercapacitor demonstrated excellent mechanical stability, retaining its performance at diverse angles and even after 4000 bending cycles. These characteristics make PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitors appropriate for wearable devices requiring flexibility, cycling stability, and mechanical toughness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flexible supercapacitor
Graphene quantum dots
Screen printing
Gel electrolyte
Polyaniline nanocomposite
Microsupercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD)
Preparation of PVA/HCl gel electrolyte and fabrication of micro supercapacitor
Effect of incorporating N-GQD in the polymer matrix

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of synthesized (A) PANI and (B) PANI/N-GQD, TEM image of (C) N-GQD, (D) PANI/N-GQD and the corresponding particle size distribution curve (inset).
(A) Raman spectrum, (B) IR spectrum, (C) fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs, (D) deconvoluted C 1 s scan, (E) N 1 s scan, and (F) O 1 s scan of NGQD.
Comparison of XRD (A), IR spectrum (B), Raman spectrum (C), and Nitrogen adsorption profile of PANI and PANI-NGQD (D).
(A) Cyclic voltammograms obtained on PANI/N-GQD electrode with different N-GQD content in the PANI matrix, and (B) corresponding areal capacitance with triplicate measurement.

Cyclic voltammograms of (A) PANI (B) PANI/N-GQD electrodes at various scan rates (2–100 mV s−1) in 1 M HCl. Comparison of areal capacitance obtained each scan rate for PANI and PANI/N-GQD electrodes (C). Impedance spectra (D), galvanostatic charge-discharge profile of PANI/N-GQD and PANI at 0.5 and 1 mA cm−2 current density (E), and the corresponding areal capacitance (F).

(A, C) Cyclic voltammogram of PANI and PANI/N-GQD based electrode at 20 mV s−1 and the capacitive contribution at the given scan rate, (B, D) Variation of capacitive and diffusion contribution towards total capacitance at different scan rates while using PANI and PANI-N-GQD respectively.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of PANI-NGQD based printed supercapacitor at different scan rates (2–100 mV s−1), (B) Areal capacitance obtained from cyclic voltammograms at various scan rates, (C) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of developed supercapacitor at various current densities ranging from (0.2–1.6 mA cm−2), (D) Variation in capacitance retention and coloumbic efficiency of the fabricated microsupercapacitor at regular interval of cycling stability test, (E) EIS response of PANI-NGQD based supercapacitor before and after 5000 cycles of potential cycling, (F) Ragone plots representation of the fabricated supercapacitor and its comparison with recent articles published in microsupercapacitor field. [34,50–56].

Cyclic voltammogram recorded on the supercapacitor (A) at various bending positions (40 mV s−1), (B) at regular intervals of bending cycles, and (C) the corresponding areal capacitance at 40 and 100 mV s−1 scan rate, Cyclic voltammograms obtained at 100 mV s−1 scan rate when the supercapacitor connected in parallel (D) and in series combination (E). Photograph of the series combination supercapacitors to power red LED and different electronic devices (F). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot embedded polyaniline for the fabrication of high-performance flexible supercapacitor with enhanced cycling stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering, Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering, Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India - 641112

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biomedical Engineering Research Centre, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amrita Biomedical Engineering Research Centre, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India - 641112

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article explains the synthesis of high surface area nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded polyaniline (PANI) and its effectiveness in fabricating flexible printed supercapacitors. A noticeable change in polyaniline's morphology and specific surface area suggests the successful incorporation of graphene quantum dots into the polymer matrix. The synergistic interaction of polyaniline with graphene quantum dots is evident in its energy storage performance. The fabricated supercapacitor prototype with PVA/HCl gel electrolyte exhibited a very high areal capacitance of 128.01 mF cm−2, with an areal energy density of 11.40 μWh cm−2, and a power density of 640 μW cm−2 at 1.6 mA cm−2 current density. Most importantly, the developed supercapacitor exhibited 76.70% of original capacitance after 5000 galvanostatic charge-discharge cycles, which was found to be far better result than a supercapacitor fabricated with pristine polyaniline. Furthermore, the developed supercapacitor demonstrated excellent mechanical stability, retaining its performance at diverse angles and even after 4000 bending cycles. These characteristics make PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitors appropriate for wearable devices requiring flexibility, cycling stability, and mechanical toughness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: An enormous boom in the development of wearable electronic devices demands small and lightweight energy storage devices. Even though the recent progress in supercapacitors was highly promising, the total volume and sandwich design of conventional supercapacitors makes them difficult to incorporate with on-chip electronic devices [1]. Micro-supercapacitors of millimeter to centimeter dimensions are emerging as a dependable power source that can directly integrate with on-chip electronic devices [2]. The successful use of micro-supercapacitors in various fields includes self-powered systems [3], implantable devices [4], the Internet of Things (IoT) [5], and wearable electronics [6] are recently reported.

Electrode design and electrode material characteristics, like specific surface area, electronic conductivity, and suitable electrolytes, determine the micro-supercapacitor performance. Various materials with an electrochemical double layer [7,8] and pseudocapacitive charge storage mechanisms [9–11] have been explored for micro-supercapacitor fabrication. Polyaniline (PANI) is distinguished from the rest of the pseudocapacitive materials due to its remarkable electrical conductivity, easy synthesis, and high yield [12]. PANI-based electrode materials are considered superior for energy storage due to their high theoretical capacitance, and reversible faradaic reactions [13,14]. The inherent flexible properties of pristine polyaniline also make it an ideal electrode material for the development of flexible supercapacitor. However, their use is restricted due to the tendency of the polymer to bulge in the presence of electrolytes and low mechanical stability. The volume changes during the doping/de-doping process cause shrinkage and swelling, which gradually affects the properties of the conducting polymer in terms of cycling stability [15]. Various methods have been explored to address these drawbacks, such as using additives during the polymerization, nanocomposite with highly conductive materials, and controlling the morphology by varying the reaction temperature [16–20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are nanoscale graphene fragments that possess unique properties that make them well-suited for energy storage applications. There are numerous advantages to utilize graphene quantum dots in supercapacitors due to their enhanced energy storage capacity, improved charge-discharge efficiency, and superior stability. Their small size and large surface area facilitate efficient ion diffusion and provide ample active sites for charge storage. Moreover, graphene quantum dots exhibit excellent electrical conductivity that enables rapid charge transfer in supercapacitor devices [21]. Recent studies suggest that the incorporation of various heteroatoms and functional groups can significantly enhance the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots, which helps extend its application in various fields [22,23]. Among these, effective nitrogen doping into the GQD (N-GQD) structure without affecting the C sp2 delocalized electronic network is found to have excellent electronic conductivity [24].

Graphene quantum dots are reported to have the property of preventing possible aggregation of polyaniline by acting like a spacer between the polymer chains. The improved structural stability of the electrode enables superior performance even after continuous charge-discharge cycles [25]. Moreover, the unique electronic structure of graphene quantum dots provides enhanced conductivity to the composite material. Also, the high surface area and wettability of the GQDs enable more active sites for charge storage and help to improve the diffusion of electrolyte ions to the electrode surface respectively [26]. In addition, some of the recent studies on conductive polymer–graphene quantum dot composite for supercapacitors shows that the electrochemical stability of GQDs are well suited to help the polymer chains to prevent its possible degradation during continuous cycling [27]. Recently researchers have also explored the combination of PANI- graphene quantum dots binary nanocomposites for enhanced cycling stability. Even though the performance of the supercapacitor with a conventional base substrate was found to be highly promising, the retention of capacitance upon mechanical deformations for wearable or flexible electronics has not been explored [28–30].

This research focuses on the synthesis and testing of polyaniline embedded with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for its potential use in printed flexible supercapacitors. The nanocomposites of polyaniline/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PANI/N-GQD) were synthesized using a chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of N-GQD. The fabricated printed symmetric supercapacitor showed high areal capacitance, energy density, and power density. The exceptional mechanical stability, flexibility, and cycling stability of the developed supercapacitor show its possibility of being incorporated into flexible and wearable electronic devices. To the best of our knowledge, no reports have been published so far on the extensive studies on the printed, flexible supercapacitor with PANI-graphene quantum dots binary nanocomposites.

The following items were bought from Aldrich: citric acid, ethylene diamine (EDA), and thiourea. Aniline (99%), hydrochloric acid (38%), and ammonium persulfate (98%) were purchased from Finar, India. Most of the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Stock solutions were made using Millipore deionized water. Conductive silver ink and carbon inks were purchased from Siltech Corporation INC, Bangalore, India. A polyethylene terephthalate sheet of 0.30 mm thickness was used as the screen printing base substrate.

A field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM - Gemini SEM 500, Zeiss) was used for the morphological study. LabRAM HR UV-VIS-NIR Raman microscope from HORIBA Jobin-Yvon (633 nm laser source) was used for the Raman spectroscopic analysis. CHI608D electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments, TX, USA) was used to characterize electrode material electrochemically and fabricated micro supercapacitor. XRD analysis was performed on the Rigaku Miniflex-II X-ray diffractometer. Thermo ScientificTM ESCALABTM Xi+ was used for x-ray photoelectron spectroscopic analysis. Fluorescence spectrum was recorded on Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Surface roughness of the printed electrode was estimated using atomic force microscope (XE-70, Park systems, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of citric acid and 0.4 mL of ethylene diamine were dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water and heated in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h [31]. The resulting solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant solution was collected and stored in the refrigerator.

To prepare PANI/N-GQD nano composite oxidative polymerization of aniline was carried out using 80 mL1.0 M HCl containing 0.1 M aniline and 1 mL of N-GQD. The reaction vessel was placed in an ice bath to maintain a temperature range of 0 °C to 4 °C. 20 mL of 0.3 M ammonium peroxy sulphate was added dropwise to the above mixture with continuous stirring. The reaction mixture was stirred for 5 h in the ice bath. The resulting solution exhibited a dark green colour and was left undisturbed overnight. The precipitate was washed 7–8 times with 1.0 M HCl and distilled water and dried overnight in an oven at 80 °C [32,33].

To assess the suitability of the PANI/N-GQD material for printed supercapacitor applications, A screen-printed three-electrode configuration was developed, as reported in our previous works [34]. The working electrode has a diameter of 2 mm and is positioned between a counter electrode and a reference electrode. The working and counter electrodes were spaced 0.5 mm apart. Before printing, PET sheets were cleaned using acetone and heated at 100 °C for 1 h.

Conducting layer of the electrode was created using commercially available silver ink, and conductive carbon ink was printed on top of it. Furthermore, Ag/AgCl was printed as the reference electrode. After each layer was printed, the electrodes underwent a curing process at 90 °C for 30 min. The same printing techniques were employed to develop a symmetric printed supercapacitor electrodes. The supercapacitor consisted of interdigitated electrodes with a constant electrode spacing of 0.15 cm. Screen printing was utilized to apply a 40:60 weight ratio of catalyst material (PANI and PANI/N-GQD) to conductive carbon ink to create the supercapacitor electrodes and modify the SPE [34]. The mass loading of the fabricated supercapacitor was found to be 2.0 mg/printed capacitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA/HCl gel electrolyte and fabrication of micro supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: PVA powder (1 g) was entirely dissolved, and a clear solution was formed in 25 mL of DI water when heated at 85 °C for 2 h. After cooling the solution, 10 mL of 3.0 M HCl was added dropwise and stirred for 4 h to promote the crosslinking reaction [35]. A uniform PVA/HCl gel layer was applied to complete the supercapacitor prototype. The gel was carefully spread across the surface to ensure an even coating. Subsequently, the prototype was left to dry naturally at room temperature for 12 h.

FESEM was used to study the two-dimensional surface morphology of the PANI and PANI/N-GQD. FESEM images confirm the interconnected polyaniline nanofibers achieved through the above-mentioned synthesis procedure (Fig. 1A). The nanofibers have an approximate average diameter of 30 nm. The micrograph demonstrates the web-like structure of interconnected nanofibers with high porosity, accounting for a high specific surface area for the material. The PANI/N-GQD composites have a characteristic flower-like structure (Fig. 1B), which shows the polymerization in the presence of quantum dots, greatly affecting the morphology. Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra and the corresponding elemental mapping of the synthesized PANI and PANI/N-GQD composite are presented in Fig. S1. Carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were found as the major proportion in PANI and PANI/N-GQD.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of synthesized N-GQD reveals the graphene quantum dots exhibited an average particle size of 4.88 nm (Fig. 1C). The TEM image of the PANI/N-GQD shows that agglomerated structure of N-GQDs are distributed on the polymer surface with an average particle size 13.11 nm (Fig. 1D).

Raman spectroscopy was used to analyze the synthesized N-GQD. The two distinct peaks at 1363 and 1607 cm−1 were attributed to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 2A). The intensity of the G band was found to be much higher than that of the D band, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.32. This shows that the synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots have sp2 hybridized carbon [36]. Further, IR spectroscopy was used to study the various functional groups present in the N-GQD (Fig. 2B). The most prominent peaks at 3079, 2903, 1648, 1552, 1473 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, CC, and NO groups respectively. The peak at 1386 cm−1 shows the CH bending vibration. UV–Vis spectrum of synthesized graphene quantum dot consists of two distinct peaks located at 240 and 344 nm (Fig.S2A), which depicts the π → π* transition associated with the aromatic domains within the GQDs and n → π* transitions caused by the various functional groups [37]. GQDs dissolved in an aqueous solution display a brown colour under ordinary lighting. However, under the irradiation of a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm, they exhibit a bright blue fluorescence (Fig.S2A). UV–Vis analysis of PANI/N-GQD nanocomposite shows two characteristic absorption bands at 355 nm (π -π*) and 445 nm (polaron - π* transition) (Fig.S2B). Unlike the UV–Vis spectra of GQD, the composite material showed a free tail which gradually increased and extended to the IR region above 800 nm, which depicts the delocalized polaron transition (π – polaron transition), showing the presence of polyaniline as emeraldine salt in the composite material [38].

N-GQD exhibited a bright blue fluorescence under UV light which can be influenced by several factors like surroundings and concentration. There are a number of factors that contribute to an increase in fluorescence intensity when GQDs are diluted with water, which includes self-quenching of GQDs at higher concentrations, enhanced solvation, and reduced aggregation [39]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig. 2C, where the excitation wavelengths varied from 300 to 450 nm, and a peak was observed at 440 nm. The excitation-dependent PL observed in GQDs is a distinctive characteristic that can be attributed to the existence of defects and various functional groups on the GQDs.

To understand the sole elemental composition of the synthesized N-GQD, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis was performed. Fig. S3 shows three predominant peaks at 285.14 eV, 399.77 eV, and 531.40 eV of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with an atomic percentage of 65.88%, 14.12%, and 20%, respectively, which indicates the successful incorporation of nitrogen in GQD [40]. The deconvoluted spectrum of carbon shows the presence of CC, CN, and CO groups at 284.63 eV, 285.87 eV, and 287.61 eV, respectively (Fig. 2D). The well-defined N 1 s spectrum reveals the existence of pyridinic nitrogen (399.75 eV), pyrolic nitrogen (400.75 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (401.81 eV) (Fig. 2E). Based on the O 1 s spectrum, as prepared NGQD contains carboxyl groups (532.2 eV) and C-O-C and/or C-OH groups (531.06 eV) (Fig. 2F).

The XRD pattern of the PANI and PANI/N-GQD (Fig. 3A) composite showed a prominent peak at 23.2° and 26.1°, respectively. The characteristic diffraction peak of the GQDs at 26° coincided with the PANI peak when they were put into the PANI matrix, creating a strong and wide peak in the case of composite. The average crystallite size of PANI, before and after the incorporation of N-GQD was analyzed using the Scherrer equation, and the values were found to be 16.059 nm and 16.082 nm, respectively. These results suggest that no new crystalline order was introduced into the composite.

Fig. 3B shows the results of an FT-IR study used to identify the functional groups in PANI and PANI/N-GQDs. The spectrum of PANI showed three distinct peaks at about 3227 cm−1, 1569 cm−1, and 1491 cm−1, which correspond to the NH stretching mode, CN stretching of benzenoid units, and CN stretching of quinoid units, respectively. The peak observed at 1290 cm−1 also indicates the CN stretching of aromatic amine observed in the benzenoid unit of PANI [41]. These results point to the existence of benzenoid and quinoid units, confirming the synthesis of PANI in its emeraldine salt form [36]. Most of the conspicuous peaks seen in PANI were preserved in the FT-IR spectra of PANI-GQDs, despite minor changes in position and intensity. Also, the additional peak at 1739 indicates the presence CO. Furthermore, the intensity of the peak at 3031 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH in N-GQDs and the NH of PANI [42].

Raman spectroscopic analysis was performed to understand the detailed insight on the interaction of polyaniline and graphene quantum dots (Fig. 3C). CH in-plane vibrations of benzenoid quinoid units show a band at 1168 cm−1. The peak at 1338 cm−1 shows the C-N+ charge delocalization of polaron structure observed in the case of emeraldine salt of polyaniline. Doublet at 1376 cm−1 indicates the CN stretching vibrations due to the presence of N-GQD. Most importantly, the intensity of all the peaks in PANI/N-GQD becomes well defined, and the intensity improved considerably, which is attributed to the growth of polyaniline structure and its interaction with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. The peaks observed at 1598 cm−1 and 1609 cm−1 indicate CC stretching of benzenoid and quinoid structure and delocalized polarons [36,42].

Nitrogen adsorption and desorption technique was used to evaluate the pore volume, pore radius, and specific surface area of PANI and PANI/N-GQD using BET and BJH method (Fig. 3D). PANI shows a very high specific surface area of 42.51 m2g−1with a pore radius of 16.98 Å and pore volume of 0.096 cc g−1. The specific surface area of polyaniline was compared with previously reported works and was found to be much higher [43–45]. This shows that the proposed synthesis procedure leads to a high surface area polyaniline structure. The addition of N-GQD further enhanced the specific surface area to 49.42 m2 g−1 with a pore radius of 17.03 Å and pore volume of 0.083 cc g−1. The high specific surface area of PANI and PANI/N-GQD provides more active sites for the electrochemical reactions and, therefore, expects a better charge storage capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incorporating N-GQD in the polymer matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical properties of PANI and PANI/N-GQD with different N-GQD content have been analyzed to find the suitable composition for better performance. For this, different volumes of N-GQD were added to the reaction mixture during the synthesis, and the obtained nanocomposites were tested using the three-electrode configuration in 1.0 M HCl as the electrolyte. From Fig. 4A, it is clear that the incorporation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots has significantly contributed to enhancing the areal capacitance. Fig. 4B shows the areal capacitance obtained on each electrode at a 40 mV s−1 scan rate. Screen printed electrode modified with pristine polyaniline exhibited an areal capacitance of 199.27 mF cm−2, and the value increased to 212.06 after the incorporation of 0.5 mL of N-GQD during the synthesis. Interestingly, 1 mL addition of N-GQD exhibited 298.70 mF cm−2, which was found to be 99.43 mF cm−2 higher than pristine polyaniline. Further, the analysis with PANI+1.25 and 1.50 mL of NGQD shows almost similar areal capacitance (306.40 and 305.39 mF cm-2, respectively) compared to PANI+1.0 mL of NGQD. The gradual decrement in areal capacitance in the case of PANI+1.75 mL N-GQD and PANI+2.0 mL NGQD is attributed to lack of ink consistency causing less amount of material being transferred at each printing. Also, the possible aggregate formation on increasing the GQD content beyond the optimum level would have caused undesirable morphology of the composite material as reported [46].

Most importantly, PANI+1.0 mL of NGQD effectively forms a stable ink formulation with conductive carbon ink and is expected to deliver high mechanical stability for the fabricated flexible printed supercapacitor. Hence, PANI +1.0 mL of N-GQD was selected for further studies.

Cyclic voltammetric analysis at different scan rates of the PANI (Fig. 5A) and PANI/N-GQD (Fig. 5B) shows the characteristic pseudocapacitive behavior with well-defined faradaic peaks. As the scan rate increased, the anodic and cathodic peaks shifted to more positive and negative potentials, respectively, due to the restricted ion availability at the electrode surface. The redox peaks were still visible despite the higher scan rates, demonstrating that both the electrodes have strong rate capability and electrochemical capacitive behavior. A linear regression coefficient (R2) of 0.99 obtained on plotting the anodic and cathodic peak currents vs scan rate shows that the charge transfer mechanism is adsorption controlled [47]. The areal capacitance of both electrodes was analyzed at various scan rates and depicted in Fig. 5C. From the graph, it is evident that the incorporation of the graphene quantum dot significantly contributed to enhancing the areal capacitance. Also, the impedance spectroscopic analysis (Fig. 5D) and the evaluation of each component in the equivalent circuit Rs(Q1(Rc(Q2(RctW)))) (Table. S1) reveal that the solution resistance and the coating resistance in both cases remain constant. Interestingly, the charge transfer resistance reduced from 94.65 Ω to 17.61 Ω after the incorporation of N-GQD in the polymer matrix. The low charge transfer resistance is attributed to the smaller size, higher surface area, and superior conductivity of the graphene quantum dot, which helps the polyaniline matrix for rapid charge transfer and ion diffusion. The reduction in Warburg resistance after incorporating N-GQD with PANI (6.79 × 10−4 Ω – 2.79 × 10−4 Ω) could be due to the possible enhancement in wettability, which helps for the excellent diffusion of electrolyte ions into the electrode structure. Hence, the overall charge storage performance of the material is expected to improve, and this is evident from the change in Q value (8.02 × 10−6-1.37 × 10−5 S sn). Further, a noticeable change in the Bode plot phase angle (−80.7 to −83.5) at a lower frequency (0.01 Hz) and knee frequency (0.0920 Hz to 1.499 Hz) suggests composite material has more capacitive behavior than pristine polyaniline. Galvanostatic charge-discharge was analyzed for both the electrodes at 0.5 and 1.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5E). The GCD curves showed symmetric triangular-shaped charge–discharge profile even at higher current densities (Fig. S4), showing good electrochemical reversibility. The PANI/N-GQD nanocomposite electrodes exhibit superior capacitance behavior and demonstrate promising charging and discharging performance compared to the PANI electrode. The areal capacitance obtained at each current density is depicted in Fig. 5F, and the improvement in areal capacitance (184.85 to 255.42 mF cm−2 at 1.0 mA cm−2 and 203.0 to 261.85 mF cm−2 at 0.5 mA cm−2) confirms the significance of N-GQD.

The charge storage mechanism of polyaniline and its performance enhancement after incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were studied using different analytical methods. The total current generated at a particular scan rate is considered as the combination of a diffusion-controlled process and current that needs to charge the double layer presenting between the electrode-electrolyte interface or the current required for initiating the faradaic reaction on the electrode. Mathematically this relationship can be represented as eqs. 1 and 2.
(1)
I p
=
I cap
+
I dif
=
av b
(2)
log
I p
= log
a + b
log
v
(3)
i
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

Based on the b value obtained (slope of log v vs log Ip), the electrochemical process involved between the electrode-electrolyte interface can be easily identified. A b value closer to 1 indicates the capacitive process, and the diffusion-limited process shows a b value near 0.5. More complex charge storage systems that undergo both capacitive and diffusion-limited processes can be addressed with the inclusion of two proportionality constants, k1, and k2, corresponding to capacitive and diffusion-limited processes (eq. 3). k1 and k2 can be easily identified by analyzing the slope (k1) intercept (k2) of v1/2 vs Ip/v1/2 [48]. The b value of PANI and PANI/N-GQD comes near 1, indicating that the capacitive reaction is more predominant than the diffusion-controlled reaction. Good capacitive materials are regarded as having less diffusion contribution even while analyzing the lower scan rates [49]. The capacitive contribution of both PANI and PANI/N-GQD electrodes at 20 mV s−1 scan rate is depicted in Fig. 6A and C, respectively. The figure shows that PANI/N-GQD possesses higher capacitive contribution than PANI-based electrodes. The variation in capacitive and diffusion contribution when changing the scan rates are shown in Fig. 6B (PANI) and Fig. 6D (PANI/N-GQD), the considerable enhancement in capacitive contribution shows the significance of N-GQD in the polymer matrix.

The electrochemical properties of the PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitor with PVA/HCl gel electrolyte were analyzed using CV, GCD, and EIS techniques and the obtained results are presented in Fig. 7 . The results show that PANI/N-GQD composite exhibits superior areal capacitance by combining electrical double-layer capacitance from GQD with extra pseudocapacitance from redox processes of PANI. The CV of the PANI/N-GQD based supercapacitor is shown in Fig. 7A. Even though the well-defined redox peaks are absent at higher scan rates, the higher current generated resulted in good areal capacitance. The superior electrochemical performance of PANI/N-GQD is attributed to a combination of factors, which include PANI/N-GQD micro- and mesopores providing high surface area and constant exposure to the electrolyte, enhancing ion transit, promoting quick redox reactions, and permitting efficient charge transfer, all of which boost areal capacitance. The areal capacitance generated at each scan rate is depicted in Fig. 7B. Decrement in areal capacitance at higher scan rates is attributed to insufficient time for electrode-ion interaction. The areal capacitance was far greater than anticipated and superior to recently published articles (Table S3), which explains the superiority of the developed polyaniline composite based microsupercapacitor. This emphasizes the significance of the synergistic relationship between PANI and N-GQDs in raising the capacitance of PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitors.

Fig. 7C shows the GCD curves at various current densities, which implies that even at higher current densities, the developed supercapacitor demonstrates excellent rate performance without any IR drop. In order to understand the role of N-GQD for cycling stability, GCD measurements were carried out for PANI and PANI/N-GQD based supercapacitor at 1.6 mA cm−2 current density (Fig. 7D). Unlike the PANI/N-GQD, the PANI-based supercapacitor showed poor cycling stability and could only retain 69.65% capacitance after 2000 cycles. In contrast, PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitors could have 94.30% of their initial capacitance even after 2000 cycles (Fig. S5). The extended cycling stability test shows that the fabricated supercapacitor exhibited 76.70% capacitance retention even after 5000 cycles with 98.64% coulombic efficiency. The performance of the supercapacitor was found to be far better than pristine polyaniline and recent literature on polyaniline nanocomposite based microsupercapacitors. A detailed comparison of the performance is given in the Table S3. Impedance analysis of the fabricated supercapacitor was performed before and after 5000 numbers of GCD cycles. The obtained data was matched with an equivalent circuit (Fig. 7E inset), and the Rct values were found to be 4.78 and 22.12 Ω, respectively. Even though the impedance changes are found to be very meager even after 5000 cycles, slight increase in Rc and decrease in Q1 indicates change in the electrode structure during the cycling stability test [57]. The variation in equivalent circuit components before and after the cycling stability test was compared and tabulated (Table S2). Ragone plot of PANI/N-GQD-based supercapacitors was augmented with comparative data from similar devices reported in other literature (Fig. 7F). The developed supercapacitor exhibits excellent energy and power density, which shows its potential for practical applications.

To prove the mechanical stability of the printed electrodes even after several bending cycles, the surface roughness and the stability of the printed electrode were estimated before and after 4000 bending cycles using atomic force microscopy and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. The change in surface roughness values of the capacitor electrodes before and after 4000 bending cycles were calculated by recording AFM images for the surface with 5 μm × 5 μm area. Three dimensional images of the surface before the cycling are presented in Fig. S6A and after cycling in Fig. S6B. The average roughness (Ra) and root mean square roughness (Rq) at 15 different areas were estimated and presented in Fig. S6 C, D, E, F. A negligible increase in Ra (from 16.49 nm to 17.08 nm) and Rq (from 20.98 nm to 21.97 nm) was observed even after 4000 bending cycles. This shows that the variation in surface texture is minimal even after 4000 bending cycles. Even though Raman spectroscopy does not provide direct information about the mechanical properties, the structural changes and the molecular vibration before and after the bending cycles can be correlated with material flexibility. The Raman spectra of the printed electrode before and after the 4000 bending cycles were found to be similar without any shift in the peak position, width of peaks, and negligible change in the peak intensities is presented in Fig.S7. The results shows that the applied strain does not have much effect on the stability of the printed layer [58,59]. Further, the strength and flexibility of the supercapacitor were analyzed by subjecting it to various bending positions and recording the cyclic voltammogram at 40 mV s−1 scan rate (Fig. 8A). Eight different bend positions were considered for the analysis, and the same current profile was observed with negligible change. Further, Fig. 8 B&C shows the CVs obtained after every 250 bending cycles up to 4000 cycles and the areal capacitance at 100 mV s−1 and 40 mV s−1 scan rates respectively. Only a negligible change in areal capacitance was observed after every 250 bending cycles with a relative standard deviation of 0.33% at 100 mV s−1 and 0.45% at 40 mV s−1. Consistent ink formulation with the nanocomposite and the flexible substrate makes the developed supercapacitor more mechanically stable. The ultra flexibility and the constant performance under continuous bending cycles show the potential application of this developed supercapacitor for flexible and wearable electronics. Further, to showcase the applicability of the developed supercapacitor, parallel and series combinations of supercapacitors were made, and the cyclic voltammograms were recorded at 100 mV s−1 scan rate. It is observed that when three cells are connected in parallel, the output current doubles compared to that of a single supercapacitor operating at the same working voltage (Fig. 8D). This demonstrates the increased capacity achieved through parallel connection. Connecting supercapacitor cells in parallel enhances the overall current-handling capability, resulting in a higher output current at the same working voltage. On the other hand, the voltage range (working window) expands when cells are connected in series. Here, the working window increases from 0.8 V for a single cell to 2.4 V for three cells connected in series (Fig. 8E). This indicates that the series connection allows for a wider voltage range, thereby enabling the supercapacitor system to store and release more energy effectively. Finally, the series combination was used to demonstrate the practical applicability. For that, the supercapacitor was charged for 10 min, and the discharge was analyzed by powering red LED (20 mA, 2 V) and different electronic devices. The red LED was glowing for 12 min, the scientific calculator (13–23 μA, 1.5 V) worked for 27 min, and the humidity meter (16.4 μA, 1.5 V) worked for 40 min (Fig. 8F).

In this work, polyaniline/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were successfully synthesized using in situ polymerization and used as electrode material for the fabrication of a flexible printed symmetric supercapacitor. The enhancement in the electrochemical property of polyaniline after the incorporation of graphene quantum dots reveals its significance in the composite material for energy storage applications. This can be attributed to the synergistic combination of N-GQDs and PANI. Unlike the polyaniline-based supercapacitors with very less cycling stability, PANI/N-GQD based supercapacitors showed exceptional capacitance retention even after 5000 charge-discharge cycles, establishing the stability of the composite material. Furthermore, the developed supercapacitor displayed exceptional retention of capacitance even when exposed to varying bending angles and 4000 bending cycles. These findings emphasize the potential use of PANI/N-GQD in wearable devices, where flexibility and mechanical robustness are crucial.

Navaneeth Punnakkal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. S Naneena: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Shyam Lal C P: Validation, Investigation. Aarathi Pradeep: Writing – review & editing. Satheesh Babu T G: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Punathil Vasu Suneesh: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Additive-free MXene inks and direct printing of micro-supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, L. McKeon, M.P. Kremer, S.-H. Park, O. Ronan, A. Seral-Ascaso, S. Barwich, C.Ó. Coileáin, N. McEvoy, H.C. Nerl, Additive-free MXene inks and direct printing of micro-supercapacitors, Nat. Commun. 10 (2019) 1795.
